approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible ways to parenthesize the matrix chain multiplication, computing the minimum cost by exploring every combination. This approach is straightforward but highly inefficient for large inputs due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursive Matrix Chain Multiplication
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that tries all possible parenthesizations to find minimum multiplication cost.
ladders.explanation: The Matrix Chain Multiplication problem asks: given a sequence of matrices with dimensions p[0..n] where matrix i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i], find the minimum number of scalar multiplications needed to compute the product A₁ × A₂ × ... × Aₙ. The brute force approach recursively explores every possible way to parenthesize the chain. Intuition: For any chain from i to j, we can split it at some k (i ≤ k < j) and recursively compute the minimum cost for both halves plus the cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices. This creates a binary tree of possibilities where each internal node represents a split point. The algorithm works because it systematically explores all (2ⁿ) possible parenthesizations, guaranteeing we find the optimal one. Step-by-step: Define function solve(i, j) that returns minimum cost for matrices i through j. Base case: if i == j, return 0 (single matrix needs no multiplication). Recursive case: For k from i to j-1, compute cost = solve(i, k) + solve(k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. Track minimum across all k. Time: O(2ⁿ) due to exponential recursion tree. Space: O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive helper function as its core component. Common mistakes: Forgetting the base case, incorrect dimension indices in the multiplication cost formula, or not handling the 1-based indexing of p array correctly. Real code pattern: def mcm_recursive(p, i, j): if i == j: return 0; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = mcm_recursive(p, i, k) + mcm_recursive(p, k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); return min_cost.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Minimum Cost Function for Matrix Subchain
ladders.desc: Recursive function that computes minimum multiplication cost for a contiguous subchain of matrices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function solve(i, j) that returns the minimum cost to multiply matrices from index i to j (1-based indexing). Intuition: Any optimal parenthesization of matrices i..j must split the chain at some point k, multiplying the optimal results from i..k and k+1..j, then multiplying those two resulting matrices. By trying all possible k, we find the minimum. The recursion naturally explores all binary trees of parenthesization. Step-by-step: If i == j, return 0 (single matrix). Otherwise, initialize min_cost = INF. For k from i to j-1: cost = solve(i, k) + solve(k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. Update min_cost. Return min_cost. Time: O(2ⁿ) in worst case as it explores all splits. Space: O(n) recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution with i=1, j=n. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, especially with p array indexing; not handling base case; using 0-based instead of 1-based matrix indices. Real code pattern: def solve(p, i, j): if i >= j: return 0; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = solve(p, i, k) + solve(p, k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; if cost < min_cost: min_cost = cost; return min_cost.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Splitting Matrix Chain and Combining Costs
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of trying all split points and combining recursive results with multiplication cost.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essential recursive relation: to find minimum cost for matrices i..j, try every possible split point k, compute cost of left part (i..k), right part (k+1..j), and the cost to multiply their resulting matrices. Intuition: Matrix multiplication is associative, so any parenthesization corresponds to a binary tree where internal nodes represent multiplications. The cost of multiplying two matrices of dimensions a×b and b×c is a*b*c scalar multiplications. By recursively applying this split-and-combine strategy, we build up solutions from smaller subchains. This is a divide-and-conquer approach without memoization, leading to exponential time. Step-by-step: Given i and j, if i == j, cost is 0. Otherwise, for each k from i to j-1: left_cost = recursively compute for i..k; right_cost = recursively compute for k+1..j; multiply_cost = p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; total = left_cost + right_cost + multiply_cost; track minimum total. Time: For each call, O(j-i) iterations; overall O(2ⁿ). Space: O(n) recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function's loop. Common mistakes: Incorrectly computing multiply_cost (must use dimensions of resulting matrices), or not considering all k values. Real code pattern: min_cost = INF; for k in range(i, j): left = recursive_call(i, k); right = recursive_call(k+1, j); current = left + right + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, current).

examples.trace: i=1,j=3. k=1: left = solve(1,1)=0; right = solve(2,3); multiply_cost = 10*30*5=1500. solve(2,3): i=2,j=3. k=2 only: left = solve(2,2)=0; right = solve(3,3)=0; multiply_cost = 30*5*5=750; total = 0+0+750=750. So right=750. Thus total for k=1: 0+750+1500=2250. k=2: left = solve(1,2); right = solve(3,3)=0; multiply_cost = 10*5*5=250. solve(1,2): i=1,j=2. k=1 only: left = solve(1,1)=0; right = solve(2,2)=0; multiply_cost = 10*30*5=1500; total=1500. So left=1500. Thus total for k=2: 1500+0+250=1750. Minimum is 1500? Wait, recalc: Actually for p=[10,30,5], matrices: A₁(10×30), A₂(30×5). Only one multiplication: 10*30*5=1500. So correct output is 1500. Trace correction: i=1,j=3 with 2 matrices? Actually n=2 matrices, so i=1,j=2. Let's do i=1,j=2: k=1 only: left=solve(1,1)=0; right=solve(2,2)=0; multiply_cost=10*30*5=1500; total=1500. Return 1500. So example should be i=1,j=2. Output 1500.

Matrices: A₁(40×20), A₂(20×30), A₃(30×10). i=1,j=3 actually since 4 dimensions mean 3 matrices. Let's correct: p has 4 elements meaning 3 matrices, so j=3. i=1,j=3. k=1: left=solve(1,1)=0; right=solve(2,3); multiply_cost=40*20*10=8000. solve(2,3): i=2,j=3. k=2 only: left=solve(2,2)=0; right=solve(3,3)=0; multiply_cost=20*30*10=6000; total=6000. So right=6000. Thus total for k=1: 0+6000+8000=14000. k=2: left=solve(1,2); right=solve(3,3)=0; multiply_cost=40*30*10=12000. solve(1,2): i=1,j=2. k=1 only: left=solve(1,1)=0; right=solve(2,2)=0; multiply_cost=40*20*30=24000; total=24000. So left=24000. Thus total for k=2: 24000+0+12000=36000. Minimum is 14000. Wait, that's not 32000. Let's recompute with correct p: Actually for output 32000, use p=[40,20,30,10,30] from earlier? But that's 5 elements. Let's use a known example: p=[40,20,30,10,30] gives 26000 for full chain. For subchain i=1,j=4 with p=[40,20,30,10,30], that's 4 matrices: A₁(40×20), A₂(20×30), A₃(30×10), A₄(10×30). Compute: k=1: left=solve(1,1)=0; right=solve(2,4); multiply_cost=40*20*30=24000. solve(2,4) from earlier example=12000. So total=0+12000+24000=36000. k=2: left=solve(1,2)=24000; right=solve(3,4)=9000; multiply_cost=40*30*30=36000; total=24000+9000+36000=69000. k=3: left=solve(1,3)=14000; right=solve(4,4)=0; multiply_cost=40*10*30=12000; total=14000+0+12000=26000. Minimum is 26000. So for this subproblem, let's define input as p=[40,20,30,10,30], i=1, j=4, output=26000. Trace as above.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Cost of Multiplying Two Matrix Products
ladders.desc: Calculate the scalar multiplication cost when multiplying two resulting matrices from subchains.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the cost of multiplying two matrices: given that the left subchain i..k produces a matrix of dimensions p[i-1] × p[k], and the right subchain k+1..j produces a matrix of dimensions p[k] × p[j], the cost to multiply them is p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j]. Intuition: When multiplying an a×b matrix with a b×c matrix, each element of the result requires b multiplications, and there are a*c elements, so total scalar multiplications are a*b*c. In the chain, the dimensions come from the p array: matrix i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i]. After multiplying i..k, the result has dimensions p[i-1] × p[k]; after multiplying k+1..j, result has dimensions p[k] × p[j]. Multiplying these gives cost p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. This is a key component in the recursive formula. Step-by-step: Given p array, indices i, k, j (1-based), ensure i ≤ k < j. Compute cost = p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j]. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This cost is added to the recursive costs in the split logic. Common mistakes: Using wrong indices (e.g., p[i]*p[k]*p[j] instead of p[i-1]), or not ensuring k is between i and j-1. Real code pattern: def multiply_cost(p, i, k, j): return p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Matrix Dimensions from Array
ladders.desc: Retrieve dimensions of matrices from the given array using proper indexing.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves understanding how matrix dimensions are stored in the p array. Given n matrices, the array p has length n+1, where matrix i (1-based) has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i]. Intuition: The dimensions are consecutive: the number of columns of matrix i equals the number of rows of matrix i+1, which is why only n+1 numbers are needed. For example, if p = [10, 30, 5], then matrix 1 is 10×30 and matrix 2 is 30×5. Accessing p[i-1] gives rows of matrix i, p[i] gives columns of matrix i. This is fundamental for all cost calculations. Step-by-step: To get dimensions of matrix i: rows = p[i-1], cols = p[i]. Ensure 1 ≤ i ≤ n. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This provides the values needed for multiply_cost. Common mistakes: Using 0-based indexing incorrectly, or accessing out of bounds. Real code pattern: rows = p[i-1]; cols = p[i].

examples.trace: For matrix 2: rows = p[1] = 30, cols = p[2] = 5. So dimensions are 30×5.
For matrix 3: rows = p[2] = 30, cols = p[3] = 10. So dimensions are 30×10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Matrix Dimensions and Multiplication Cost
ladders.desc: Learn how matrix dimensions determine multiplication cost and the associative property.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers two key concepts: 1) Matrix dimensions: A matrix with m rows and n columns is an m×n matrix. When multiplying two matrices A (m×n) and B (n×p), the result is an m×p matrix. The multiplication is valid only if number of columns of A equals number of rows of B. 2) Multiplication cost: To compute each element of the result, we do n multiplications (dot product of row from A and column from B). There are m*p elements, so total scalar multiplications are m*n*p. For example, multiplying 2×3 and 3×4 matrices costs 2*3*4=24. 3) Associativity: Matrix multiplication is associative: (AB)C = A(BC), but the cost may differ based on parenthesization. Intuition: Changing parentheses changes intermediate matrix sizes, affecting total multiplications. This is why we need to find optimal parenthesization. Step-by-step: Given dimensions, verify compatibility, then compute cost. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This explains why the p array stores dimensions as consecutive numbers. Common mistakes: Confusing rows and columns, or not checking compatibility. Real code pattern: if A_cols != B_rows: error; else cost = A_rows * A_cols * B_cols.

examples.trace: A is 2×3, B is 3×4. Compatibility: A columns (3) equals B rows (3), so valid. Cost = 2 * 3 * 4 = 24.
A is 10×30, B is 30×5. Compatibility: 30 equals 30, valid. Cost = 10 * 30 * 5 = 1500.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursive DP O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization (top-down dynamic programming) to store and reuse results of subproblems, avoiding redundant calculations. This approach improves efficiency by caching intermediate results while maintaining a recursive structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Matrix Chain Multiplication with Memoized Recursive DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using memoized recursion to find minimum scalar multiplications for matrix chain multiplication.
ladders.explanation: The Matrix Chain Multiplication problem involves finding the optimal parenthesization of a chain of matrices to minimize the number of scalar multiplications. Given an array p[] where matrix A_i has dimensions p[i-1] x p[i], we need to compute the minimum cost of multiplying the chain from matrix i to j. The intuition is that any parenthesization splits the chain at some point k, and the total cost is the sum of costs of multiplying the left and right subchains plus the cost of multiplying their results. This exhibits optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems, making it suitable for dynamic programming. The memoized recursive approach (top-down DP) uses recursion to explore all possible splits, but caches results in a memo table to avoid redundant calculations. The algorithm defines a recursive function solve(i, j) that returns the minimum cost to multiply matrices from i to j. Base cases: if i == j, cost is 0 (single matrix). Otherwise, iterate k from i to j-1, compute cost = solve(i, k) + solve(k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j], and take the minimum. Store results in memo[i][j]. Time complexity is O(n^3) due to three nested loops in recursion (i, j, k), and space is O(n^2) for the memo table. This feeds into the full problem by directly implementing the complete solution. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases, off-by-one errors in array indices, and not initializing memo properly. Real code pattern: def mcm_memo(p, i, j, memo): if i == j: return 0; if memo[i][j] != -1: return memo[i][j]; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = mcm_memo(p, i, k, memo) + mcm_memo(p, k+1, j, memo) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); memo[i][j] = min_cost; return min_cost.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization for Subproblem Cost
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes minimum cost for a subchain using memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the memoized DP approach. It defines a function solve(i, j) that returns the minimum scalar multiplications needed to multiply matrices from index i to j (1-indexed). The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems by trying all possible split points k between i and j-1. For each split, the cost is the sum of costs for left subchain (i to k), right subchain (k+1 to j), and the cost to multiply the resulting matrices. This recursive definition leverages optimal substructure. Memoization is used to store computed results in a 2D array memo[i][j] to avoid recomputation, turning exponential time into polynomial. The algorithm: if i == j, return 0 (base case). If memo[i][j] is already computed, return it. Initialize min_cost to infinity. For k from i to j-1: cost = solve(i, k) + solve(k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. Update min_cost. Store min_cost in memo[i][j] and return it. Time complexity for this function in isolation is O(n^3) when called for the full chain, as it explores O(n^2) subproblems and each takes O(n) for splits. Space is O(n^2) for memo. This feeds into L0 by being the core recursive function called to solve the full problem. Common mistakes: forgetting base cases, incorrect memo lookup, or not handling indices properly. Real code pattern: def solve(p, i, j, memo): if i == j: return 0; if memo[i][j] != -1: return memo[i][j]; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = solve(p, i, k, memo) + solve(p, k+1, j, memo) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); memo[i][j] = min_cost; return min_cost.

examples.trace: Call solve(1,3). i=1, j=3, not base case. Check memo[1][3] (assume uninitialized). min_cost = inf. k=1: cost = solve(1,1)+solve(2,3)+10*30*60. solve(1,1): i=1,j=1, base case return 0. solve(2,3): i=2,j=3. k=2: cost = solve(2,2)+solve(3,3)+30*5*60=0+0+9000=9000. So solve(2,3)=9000. Thus k=1 cost=0+9000+18000=27000. min_cost=27000. k=2: cost = solve(1,2)+solve(3,3)+10*5*60. solve(1,2): i=1,j=2. k=1: cost = solve(1,1)+solve(2,2)+10*30*5=0+0+1500=1500. So solve(1,2)=1500. Thus k=2 cost=1500+0+3000=4500. min_cost=min(27000,4500)=4500. Store memo[1][3]=4500. Return 4500.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Split Point Selection with Cost Calculation
ladders.desc: Determine the optimal split point k and compute cost for multiplying subchains.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves iterating through all possible split points k between i and j-1 to find the one that minimizes total cost. The intuition is that any parenthesization of matrices i to j must multiply some product of matrices i to k with matrices k+1 to j, so we try every k to find the best. For each k, the cost is computed as: cost = cost(i, k) + cost(k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j], where cost(i, k) and cost(k+1, j) are minimum costs for subchains, and p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] is the cost to multiply the two resulting matrices. This formula arises because if matrix A_i has dimensions p[i-1] x p[i], then the product of matrices i to k has dimensions p[i-1] x p[k], and product of k+1 to j has dimensions p[k] x p[j], so multiplying them costs p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. The algorithm: initialize min_cost to infinity. For k from i to j-1: compute cost using the formula, update min_cost. This is the central loop in the recursive function. Time complexity for this logic alone is O(n) per subproblem, as it loops over k. Space is O(1). This feeds into L1 by being the key step inside the recursive function to compute cost for a given i and j. Common mistakes: incorrect indices in p array, off-by-one in loop range, or misunderstanding the cost formula. Real code pattern: min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = left_cost + right_cost + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost).

examples.input: p = [10, 30, 5, 60], i=1, j=3, left_cost=0 for k=1, right_cost=9000 for k=1
p = [40, 20, 30, 10, 30], i=2, j=4, left_cost=0 for k=2, right_cost=9000 for k=2

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Cost Calculation for Subchains
ladders.desc: Compute minimum cost for multiplying a subchain recursively without memoization.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves writing a recursive function to compute the minimum cost for multiplying matrices from i to j, without using memoization. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems by recursively calculating costs for left and right subchains. This demonstrates the naive recursive approach that has exponential time due to overlapping subproblems. The algorithm: define function cost(i, j). If i == j, return 0. Initialize min_cost to infinity. For k from i to j-1: min_cost = min(min_cost, cost(i, k) + cost(k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]). Return min_cost. This directly implements the recurrence relation. Time complexity is exponential, O(2^n), because each call branches into two subcalls. Space is O(n) for recursion stack. This feeds into L2 by showing the recursive structure that is later optimized with memoization. Common mistakes: missing base case, infinite recursion, or incorrect cost formula. Real code pattern: def cost(p, i, j): if i == j: return 0; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): min_cost = min(min_cost, cost(p, i, k) + cost(p, k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]); return min_cost.

examples.trace: Call cost(1,2). i=1, j=2, not base case. k=1 only: cost = cost(1,1)+cost(2,2)+10*30*5. cost(1,1): i=1,j=1, base return 0. cost(2,2): base return 0. So cost = 0+0+1500=1500. min_cost=1500. Return 1500.
Call cost(3,4). i=3, j=4. k=3 only: cost = cost(3,3)+cost(4,4)+30*10*30. cost(3,3): base 0. cost(4,4): base 0. So cost = 0+0+9000=9000. min_cost=9000. Return 9000.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matrix Multiplication Cost for Two Matrices
ladders.desc: Calculate the cost (scalar multiplications) to multiply two matrices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the number of scalar multiplications required to multiply two matrices. Given matrices A of dimensions a x b and B of dimensions b x c, the cost is a * b * c. The intuition is that matrix multiplication involves computing each element of the result matrix as a dot product, requiring b multiplications per element, and there are a * c elements total. This is a fundamental operation in matrix chain multiplication, as it represents the cost of multiplying two subchains. The algorithm: simply return a * b * c. Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L3 by being used in the cost formula for splitting subchains. Common mistakes: confusing dimensions or using wrong values. Real code pattern: def multiply_cost(a, b, c): return a * b * c.

examples.trace: Cost = 10 * 30 * 5 = 1500. This means multiplying a 10x30 matrix with a 30x5 matrix requires 1500 scalar multiplications.
Cost = 30 * 5 * 60 = 9000. Multiplying a 30x5 matrix with a 5x60 matrix requires 9000 scalar multiplications.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Matrix Dimensions and Multiplication
ladders.desc: Learn how matrix dimensions determine multiplication compatibility and cost.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of matrix multiplication. A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers with rows and columns. Matrix A with dimensions m x n has m rows and n columns. Two matrices A (m x n) and B (n x p) can be multiplied if the number of columns in A equals the number of rows in B. The result matrix C has dimensions m x p. Each element C[i][j] is computed as the dot product of row i of A and column j of B: sum_{k=1 to n} A[i][k] * B[k][j]. The number of scalar multiplications required is m * n * p, because each of the m * p elements requires n multiplications. This is crucial for Matrix Chain Multiplication, as the cost depends on these dimensions. The intuition is that larger dimensions increase cost, and chain multiplication order affects total cost. Time to compute one multiplication is O(m*n*p), but here we only care about the count. Space is O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing the basis for the cost calculation. Common mistakes: assuming matrices can be multiplied in any order or misidentifying dimensions. Real code pattern: just understand that for matrices A (a x b) and B (b x c), cost = a * b * c.

examples.trace: A has 2 rows, 3 columns. B has 3 rows, 4 columns. Since columns of A (3) equals rows of B (3), they can be multiplied. Result matrix will have 2 rows and 4 columns. Cost = 2 * 3 * 4 = 24 scalar multiplications.
A is 5x2, B is 2x6. Columns of A (2) equals rows of B (2), so compatible. Result is 5x6. Cost = 5 * 2 * 6 = 60 multiplications.

approaches.name: Iterative DP Bottom-Up O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Solve the problem iteratively using a bottom-up dynamic programming table to compute the minimum multiplication cost for all subchains. This method builds solutions from smaller subproblems to larger ones without recursion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Matrix Chain Multiplication Cost
ladders.desc: Complete iterative DP solution to find minimum multiplication cost for matrix chain multiplication.
ladders.explanation: The Matrix Chain Multiplication problem aims to find the optimal parenthesization of a chain of matrices to minimize the total number of scalar multiplications. Given an array p[] where matrix A_i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i], we need to compute the minimum cost to multiply the chain A_1A_2...A_n. The iterative bottom-up DP approach builds a 2D table dp[i][j] representing the minimum cost to multiply matrices from i to j. We fill this table diagonally, starting with subchains of length 2 up to length n. For each subchain, we try all possible split points k between i and j-1, computing cost = dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j], and take the minimum. The intuition is that optimal parenthesization of a chain can be found by combining optimal solutions of its subchains, following the optimal substructure property. Time complexity is O(n³) due to three nested loops, and space is O(n²) for the DP table. This approach avoids recursion overhead and builds solutions systematically from smaller to larger subproblems. Common mistakes include incorrect index handling (especially with 0-based vs 1-based indexing), forgetting to initialize dp[i][i] = 0 (cost of single matrix is 0), and incorrect loop boundaries. The solution connects to L1 by using the split point evaluation as the key subroutine.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Evaluating Split Points for Minimum Cost
ladders.desc: Compute minimum cost for a given matrix subchain by trying all split points.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the MCM DP solution. Given a subchain of matrices from index i to j (i < j), we need to find the optimal split point k (i ≤ k < j) that minimizes the total multiplication cost. The cost for a split at k is: cost = dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j], where dp[i][k] is the minimum cost to multiply matrices i to k, dp[k+1][j] is for k+1 to j, and p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] is the cost to multiply the two resulting matrices. The intuition is that any parenthesization of the chain (i...j) must have a top-level multiplication between some left group (i...k) and right group (k+1...j). By trying all possible k, we find the one that yields the minimum total cost, leveraging optimal solutions for subchains. The algorithm: initialize min_cost = INF, for k from i to j-1: compute current_cost = dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j], update min_cost if current_cost is smaller. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(j-i) per subchain, and overall O(n³) when integrated. Space is O(1) extra. This feeds into L0 by being called for each subchain during DP table filling. Common mistakes: using wrong indices for p (remember p indices are 0-based but i,j are 1-based for matrix numbering), or incorrect loop range for k. Real pattern: for k in range(i, j): cost = dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Programming Table Filling Strategy
ladders.desc: Fill DP table diagonally for increasing subchain lengths.
ladders.explanation: The core technique in iterative MCM is filling a 2D DP table in a specific order to ensure smaller subproblems are solved before larger ones. We define dp[i][j] as the minimum multiplication cost for matrices i to j (1-based indexing). The table is filled diagonally: first all subchains of length 1 (dp[i][i] = 0), then length 2, length 3, up to length n. For each length L from 2 to n, we iterate i from 1 to n-L+1, set j = i+L-1, and compute dp[i][j] using the split point subroutine from L1. The intuition is that to compute cost for a chain of length L, we need costs for all smaller subchains (length < L), which are already computed due to the filling order. This bottom-up approach builds solutions incrementally, avoiding recursion and ensuring each subproblem is solved only once. Time complexity arises from three loops: L (n-1 times), i (n-L+1 times), and k (up to L-1 times), totaling O(n³). Space is O(n²) for the table. This connects to L1 by using the subroutine for each (i,j) pair. Common mistakes: incorrect loop boundaries leading to index errors, or filling table row-wise/column-wise which doesn't guarantee subproblems are solved. Real pattern: for L in range(2, n+1): for i in range(1, n-L+2): j = i+L-1; dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] for k in range(i, j)).

ladders.title: Building Block — Matrix Multiplication Cost Formula
ladders.desc: Compute cost of multiplying two matrices given their dimensions.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to MCM: given two matrices A (dimensions a×b) and B (dimensions b×c), the cost (number of scalar multiplications) to multiply them is a * b * c. This arises because matrix multiplication involves computing each element of the result as a dot product: for a resulting matrix of size a×c, each element requires b multiplications, so total multiplications are a * b * c. In MCM, when we have a chain of matrices, the cost of multiplying two subchains depends on the dimensions of the first and last matrices in each subchain. Specifically, if we multiply a chain resulting in a matrix of dimensions p[i-1] × p[k] with another resulting in p[k] × p[j], the cost is p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j]. The intuition is that the inner dimension (p[k]) must match for multiplication to be valid, and the outer dimensions determine the size of the result. This formula is used in the DP recurrence. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L2 as it's part of the cost computation during DP table filling. Common mistakes: confusing dimensions (e.g., using wrong indices), or misunderstanding that cost is per multiplication not additions. Real pattern: cost = rows_of_first * cols_of_first * cols_of_second, where cols_of_first = rows_of_second.

examples.trace: Cost = 2 * 3 * 4 = 24. Because A has 2 rows and 3 columns, B has 3 rows and 4 columns. Result matrix is 2x4, each element requires 3 multiplications, total 2*4*3=24.
Cost = 10 * 20 * 30 = 6000. Result matrix is 10x30, each element: 20 multiplications, total 10*30*20=6000.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Subchain Indices
ladders.desc: Generate all valid (i,j) pairs for matrix subchains.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves creating all possible contiguous subchains of matrices in a chain. Given n matrices, we need to consider subchains from index i to j where 1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n. In iterative DP, we fill these in order of increasing length. The algorithm uses nested loops: outer loop for length L from 1 to n, inner loop for start index i from 1 to n-L+1, then compute j = i+L-1. This ensures we cover all subchains without repetition and in increasing size. The intuition is that to solve for larger chains, we need solutions for all smaller contiguous subchains first. Time complexity for generating all pairs is O(n²) if done naively, but in DP it's integrated into O(n³). Space is O(1) extra. This connects to L3 by providing the (i,j) pairs for which we compute multiplication costs. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, or missing subchains of length 1. Real pattern: for L in range(1, n+1): for i in range(1, n-L+2): j = i+L-1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Matrix Dimensions Array
ladders.desc: Interpret array p[] as dimensions of matrices in a chain.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for MCM: representing a chain of matrices using an array p[] of size n+1, where matrix A_i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i] for i from 1 to n. For example, if p = [10, 20, 30], then there are 2 matrices: A_1 is 10×20 and A_2 is 20×30. The array encodes that consecutive matrices share a dimension: the number of columns of A_i equals the number of rows of A_{i+1}. This representation is compact and essential for computing multiplication costs. The intuition is that matrix multiplication is only defined when the inner dimensions match, and p[] captures this matching implicitly. Time and space are O(1) to interpret. This connects to L4 as p[] is used to generate subchain indices. Common mistakes: misindexing (0-based vs 1-based), or misunderstanding that p has n+1 elements for n matrices. Real pattern: Given p, matrix i has rows = p[i-1], cols = p[i].

examples.trace: n = len(p)-1 = 2 matrices. Matrix 1: dimensions p[0]×p[1] = 40×20. Matrix 2: dimensions p[1]×p[2] = 20×30.
